skill.name: microscopy-scale-bar-adder
skill.description: Add accurate, publication-ready scale bars to microscopy images given pixel-to-unit calibration data.
skill.summary: Adds calibrated scale bars to microscopy images using Pillow for publication-ready figures.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 86 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 11 path traversal detections reference the skill's own built-in path traversal protection function in scripts/main.py, not an actual vulnerability. The 9 weak cryptographic algorithm flags match documentation text and argparse setup lines, not cryptographic code. The 59 shell execution flags are markdown code examples in SKILL.md, not active command execution. The 2 hardcoded URL findings point to the skill's own GitHub repository. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection was detected. The skill is a legitimate scientific image processing tool that has been professionally audited with a score of 89/100.

content.user_title: Add Scale Bars to Microscopy Images for Publications
content.value_statement: Microscopy images often lack accurate scale bars needed for publication. This skill adds calibrated scale bars to microscopy images using pixel-to-unit data and Pillow image processing.
actual_capabilities: Adds a calibrated scale bar to microscopy images (TIFF, PNG, JPG, BMP) with specified length and unit
Reads TIFF XResolution metadata automatically for pixel-to-unit calibration
Supports four scale bar positions: bottom-right, bottom-left, top-right, top-left
Configurable bar and label colors, bar thickness, and output file path
Preserves original image resolution and metadata in the output
Validates paths to block directory traversal attempts
limitations: Cannot add scale bars without calibration data (pixels-per-unit or TIFF XResolution)
Does not extract scale values from image content or existing annotations
Scale bar label uses a default bitmap font when no system font is available
Only supports rectangular scale bars, not custom shapes or patterns

use_cases.title: Add scale bar to fluorescence microscopy image
use_cases.description: A researcher prepares a fluorescence microscopy image for journal submission and needs a 10 micrometer scale bar.

use_cases.title: Batch process images with consistent bar styling
use_cases.description: A lab technician processes a set of TIFF images and needs uniform scale bar appearance across all files.

use_cases.title: Verify scale bar accuracy against known calibration
use_cases.description: A microscopy core facility staff member verifies that scale bars match the known calibration of the imaging system.
use_cases.target_user: Core facility staff verifying image outputs

prompt_templates.title: Basic scale bar addition
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a 50 um scale bar to image.tif using the default bottom-right position.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a single TIFF image and needs a quick scale bar.

prompt_templates.title: Custom scale bar with position and color
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a 10 um scale bar to image.tif at the bottom-left with a yellow bar and black label.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a scale bar that contrasts with the image background.

prompt_templates.title: Scale bar using TIFF metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the XResolution from image.tif and add a 5 um scale bar. Save the output as annotated.tif.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a TIFF with embedded calibration metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Scale bar with nanometer unit
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a 100 nm scale bar to the TEM image micrograph.tif. The image calibration is 2.5 pixels per nanometer.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs a nanometer-scale bar for an electron microscopy image.

output_examples.input: Add a 50 um scale bar to slide.tif using pixels-per-unit 5.0
output_examples.output: Saved: slide_scalebar.tif (bar=250px, 50.0 um). The image now has a white scale bar 250 pixels long at the bottom-right corner with the label 50.0 um above it.

output_examples.input: Add a 10 um scale bar at bottom-left with a yellow bar
output_examples.output: Saved: output_with_scalebar.png (bar=50px, 10.0 um). The image has a yellow scale bar 50 pixels long positioned at the bottom-left corner.

best_practices: Always verify that pixels-per-unit calibration matches your imaging system before adding a scale bar.
Use TIFF format when possible to preserve image metadata including XResolution calibration.
Choose bar and label colors that contrast with the image background for clear visibility.
anti_patterns: Do not guess or fabricate scale calibration values. The skill requires accurate calibration data to produce correct scale bars.
Do not use the scale bar skill for non-microscopy images or tasks like image segmentation or cell counting.
Do not provide paths with ../ sequences. Use flat file paths or paths within your workspace directory.

faq.question: What image formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: TIFF, PNG, JPG, and BMP formats are supported. TIFF images can provide automatic calibration from XResolution metadata.

faq.question: How does the skill determine the scale bar length?
faq.answer: The skill multiplies the requested physical length by the pixels-per-unit calibration. It can read this from TIFF XResolution metadata or accept it as a command parameter.

faq.question: What happens if the image has no calibration metadata?
faq.answer: The skill asks for a pixels-per-unit value. This value is required when TIFF XResolution data is not available.

faq.question: Can I customize the scale bar appearance?
faq.answer: Yes. You can set bar and label colors, bar thickness in pixels, and choose from four positions: bottom-right, bottom-left, top-right, and top-left.

faq.question: Does the skill modify the original image file?
faq.answer: No. The skill saves the annotated image to a new file. The default output name adds underscore scalebar to the input filename.

faq.question: What fonts are used for the scale bar label?
faq.answer: The skill tries to use a system font. If no font is found, it falls back to the Pillow default bitmap font for the label text.
